name: Probability & Statistics
description: The math of uncertainty. From basic probability to Monte Carlo simulations — the tools every trader and data thinker needs.

nodes.name: Probability Foundations
nodes.description: Events, sample spaces, independence, and conditional probability. The language of uncertainty.
nodes.whyItMatters: Every trade is a bet with uncertain outcomes. Probability is how you quantify uncertainty. Without it, you're guessing. With it, you're calculating.
practiceProblems: Roll two dice. What's P(sum = 7)?
A deck has 52 cards. P(drawing a heart | card is red)?
If a test is 95% accurate and 1% have the disease, what's P(disease | positive)?
Two coin flips. Are they independent?

resources.title: Khan Academy: Probability
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy's complete probability course — events, conditional probability, Bayes' theorem. Master the math of uncertainty with interactive exercises.

resources.title: Conditional Probability Visualized
resources.whyHelpful: 3Blue1Brown's visual explanation of conditional probability and Bayes' theorem. Intuitive understanding of the most important formula in probability.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Binomial Distributions
resources.whyHelpful: 3Blue1Brown's elegant visual guide to binomial distributions — the foundation for understanding trading outcomes and risk over multiple trials.

quiz.question: You roll two fair dice. What's the probability of getting a sum of 7?
quiz.explanation: There are 6 ways to get 7 (1+6, 2+5, 3+4, 4+3, 5+2, 6+1) out of 36 total outcomes. 6/36 = 1/6.

quiz.question: If P(A) = 0.3 and P(B) = 0.4, and A and B are independent, what's P(A and B)?
quiz.explanation: For independent events, P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) = 0.3 × 0.4 = 0.12.

quiz.question: A test is 95% accurate and 1% of people have the disease. You test positive. What's the approximate probability you actually have the disease?
quiz.explanation: This is the base rate fallacy. Using Bayes' theorem: (0.95 × 0.01) / (0.95 × 0.01 + 0.05 × 0.99) ≈ 16%. Most positives are false positives because the disease is rare.

quiz.question: Two coins are flipped. Given that at least one is heads, what's the probability both are heads?
quiz.explanation: Possible outcomes given at least one heads: HH, HT, TH. Only HH has both heads. So P = 1/3.

workedExamples.problem: You roll two fair six-sided dice. What is the probability that the sum is 7 or 11?
workedExamples.explanation: When events are mutually exclusive (can't happen at the same time), you add their probabilities. There are 6 ways to roll 7 and 2 ways to roll 11 out of 36 total outcomes.

workedExamples.problem: You draw 2 cards from a standard 52-card deck without replacement. What is the probability both are aces? What is the probability at least one is an ace?
workedExamples.explanation: Without replacement, the second draw depends on the first. For \"at least one\" problems, it's often easier to calculate the complement (none) and subtract from 1. This is the complement rule: P(at least one) = 1 - P(none).

workedExamples.problem: You make 5 independent trades. Each has a 40% win rate. What is the probability you win at least 3? What does this tell us about trading with sub-50% win rates?
workedExamples.solution: This is a binomial: X ~ Binomial(n=5, p=0.4). P(X=3) = C(5,3) × 0.4³ × 0.6² = 10 × 0.064 × 0.36 = 0.2304. P(X=4) = C(5,4) × 0.4⁴ × 0.6¹ = 5 × 0.0256 × 0.6 = 0.0768. P(X=5) = 0.4⁵ = 0.0102. P(X≥3) = 0.2304 + 0.0768 + 0.0102 = 0.3174 (31.7%). Even with a 40% win rate, only about 1 in 3 days of 5 trades will have 3+ wins. This is why risk management and position sizing matter more than win rate alone.
workedExamples.explanation: Even with a losing win rate (40%), you can still be profitable if your average win is larger than your average loss. This is why traders focus on expected value: win_rate × avg_win - loss_rate × avg_loss. If avg_win = $300 and avg_loss = $150, EV = 0.4×$300 - 0.6×$150 = $30 per trade — positive despite losing more often than winning.

commonMistakes: Adding probabilities of non-mutually-exclusive events without subtracting the overlap — use P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B).
Assuming past rolls affect future rolls (gambler's fallacy) — dice have no memory.
Counting outcomes incorrectly — (1,2) and (2,1) are different outcomes when rolling two dice.

nodes.name: Random Variables & Distributions
nodes.description: Discrete vs continuous variables, PMF, PDF, CDF, mean, variance, and standard deviation.
keyConcepts: Expected value E[X] and variance Var(X)
nodes.whyItMatters: Random variables let you model anything uncertain — stock returns, trade outcomes, dice rolls. Distributions describe the shape of that uncertainty.
practiceProblems: Fair die roll: E[X] and Var(X)?
Given PDF f(x)=2x on [0,1], find P(X < 0.5)
What's the difference between PMF and PDF?
If E[X]=5 and Var(X)=4, what are the units of each?

resources.title: Khan Academy: Random Variables
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy's course on random variables — PMF, CDF, expected value, and variance with clear examples and practice problems.

resources.title: Seeing Theory: Probability Distributions (Interactive)
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive visualizations of probability distributions from Brown University. Adjust parameters and see distributions change in real time.

resources.title: StatLect: Random Variables
resources.whyHelpful: StatLect's rigorous guide to random variables — PMF, PDF, expected value, and variance with detailed mathematical derivations and examples.

quiz.question: What is the expected value of a fair six-sided die roll?
Which distribution models the number of successes in a fixed number of independent trials?
quiz.explanation: The Binomial distribution models the number of successes in n independent Bernoulli trials with the same probability of success.

quiz.question: What is the key difference between a PMF and a PDF?
options: PMF is for continuous variables, PDF for discrete
PMF gives probabilities directly, PDF gives density (not direct probability)
PMF is for normal distributions only
quiz.explanation: PMF (Probability Mass Function) gives exact probabilities for discrete variables. PDF (Probability Density Function) gives density; probability is the area under the curve.

workedExamples.problem: A random variable X represents the number of heads in 3 fair coin flips. What is the PMF of X, and what are E[X] and Var(X)?
workedExamples.explanation: For a discrete random variable, the PMF gives the probability of each possible value. Expected value is the probability-weighted average. Variance measures spread around the mean.

workedExamples.problem: Your trading strategy has a 40% win rate. You take 20 trades per day. Let X be the number of winning trades in a day. What distribution does X follow? Find E[X], Var(X), and P(X ≥ 10).
workedExamples.explanation: The binomial distribution models the number of successes in n independent trials. For a trader, this tells you how many winning trades to expect in a day and the probability of having a really good day (10+ wins). Most days (75.5%) you'll have 9 or fewer wins.

workedExamples.problem: You measure daily returns of ETH over 100 days. The mean return is 0.1% per day with standard deviation 2.5%. Model returns as a continuous random variable. What is the probability of a return between -2% and +3%? What is the probability of a loss greater than 5%?
workedExamples.explanation: The normal distribution approximates many financial variables. Converting to Z-scores standardizes any normal distribution, letting you use standard tables. The tail probability (2.07%) tells you how often to expect large losses — crucial for risk planning. But remember: real returns have fat tails, so actual extreme events are more common than the normal predicts.

commonMistakes: Confusing PMF with PDF — PMF is for discrete variables (gives exact probabilities), PDF is for continuous (gives density, not direct probability).
Forgetting that variance is E[X²] - (E[X])², not just E[X²] — this is the most common variance calculation error.
Treating sample statistics as population parameters — sample mean estimates the true mean but is not equal to it.

nodes.name: Common Distributions
nodes.description: Normal, binomial, uniform, exponential, Poisson, and log-normal. The distributions that show up everywhere.
nodes.whyItMatters: Stock returns are often modeled as log-normal. Trade win counts are binomial. Waiting times between events are exponential. Know your distributions or model the world wrong.
practiceProblems: 10 trades, 60% win rate. P(exactly 7 wins)?
Why are stock prices log-normal but returns normal?
Average 2 trades per day. P(0 trades today)?

resources.title: Khan Academy: Normal Distribution
resources.whyHelpful: Structured progression with practice problems on normal distributions, Z-scores, and the 68-95-99.7 rule. Interactive exercises with instant feedback. Best starting point for the fundamentals.

resources.title: Bayes theorem, the geometry of changing beliefs
resources.whyHelpful: Beautiful visual proof of Bayes theorem showing how new evidence shifts probability distributions. Makes the abstract formula intuitive through geometry.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Normal Distribution
resources.whyHelpful: Visual intuition for why normal distributions appear everywhere in nature. Explains the bell curve shape and why so many phenomena follow this pattern.

quiz.question: If P(A|B) = P(A), what does this tell us?
options: A and B are mutually exclusive
B is a subset of A
quiz.explanation: If P(A|B) = P(A), knowing B occurred does not change the probability of A. This is the definition of independence.

quiz.question: What is Bayes Theorem used for?
options: Updating probabilities based on new evidence
quiz.explanation: Bayes Theorem allows you to update the probability of a hypothesis as new evidence becomes available.

quiz.question: What is the 68-95-99.7 rule for normal distributions?
quiz.explanation: For a normal distribution, ~68% of data falls within 1 standard deviation of the mean, ~95% within 2σ, and ~99.7% within 3σ.

workedExamples.problem: A stock's daily returns are normally distributed with mean 0.05% and standard deviation 1.2%. What is the probability of a return greater than 2%? What about less than -3%?
workedExamples.explanation: Standardizing converts any normal distribution to Z-scores. The standard normal table (or 68-95-99.7 rule) then gives probabilities. Extreme moves are much rarer than people intuitively expect.

workedExamples.problem: You make 50 independent trades, each with a 60% win rate. Use the binomial distribution to find P(exactly 30 wins), P(at least 25 wins), and P(fewer than 20 wins). Why might the normal approximation be useful here?
workedExamples.solution: X ~ Binomial(50, 0.6). E[X] = 30, Var(X) = 12, σ ≈ 3.46. P(X=30) ≈ C(50,30) × 0.6³° × 0.4²° ≈ 0.1146 (11.5%). P(X≥25) ≈ 0.94 (using normal approx with continuity correction). P(X<20) ≈ 0.0033 (0.33%). Normal approximation works because np=30>5 and n(1-p)=20>5.
workedExamples.explanation: The normal approximation to the binomial saves computation for large n. With 50 trades, calculating exact binomial probabilities is tedious. The approximation gets better as n increases. This is how you quickly estimate the probability of various win-count outcomes.

workedExamples.problem: Stock prices are often modeled as log-normal, while returns are modeled as normal. If a stock starts at $100 and daily log-returns are N(0.0004, 0.02²), what is the expected price after 30 days? What is the median price? Why do they differ?
workedExamples.solution: After 30 days, cumulative log-return ~ N(30×0.0004, 30×0.02²) = N(0.012, 0.01095). E[S₃₀] = 100 × exp(0.012 + 0.01095/2) = 100 × exp(0.01748) ≈ $101.76. Median = 100 × exp(0.012) ≈ $101.21. The expected value exceeds the median because the log-normal distribution is right-skewed — a few very high prices pull the mean up.
workedExamples.explanation: Log-normal prices model the fact that prices can't go below zero but can go arbitrarily high. The right skew means the average outcome is higher than the typical (median) outcome. This is why most traders underperform the average — they experience the median, not the mean.

commonMistakes: Assuming all financial data is normally distributed — returns have fat tails (more extreme events than normal predicts).
Using the 68-95-99.7 rule for precise calculations — it's an approximation; use Z-tables for exact values.
Confusing standard deviation with standard error — standard deviation describes the population; standard error describes the sample mean's uncertainty.

nodes.name: Expected Value & Risk
nodes.description: EV calculations, law of large numbers, variance as risk, and risk-neutral thinking.
keyConcepts: Expected value formula: E[X] = Σ x·P(x)
Variance and standard deviation as risk measures
nodes.whyItMatters: Every trade has an expected value. Positive EV trades make money over time. Negative EV trades bleed you dry. This is the mathematical definition of a good bet vs a bad bet.
practiceProblems: Coin flip: win $10 on heads, lose $8 on tails. EV?
You take a trade with 40% win rate, avg win $300, avg loss $150. EV per trade?
After 100 trades, how close to theoretical EV?
Game costs $5, 1% chance to win $400. Should you play?

resources.title: Expected Value Explained
resources.whyHelpful: Clear introduction to expected value with visual examples. Builds the intuition for why +EV bets make money over time.

resources.title: Expected Value & Variance (Khan Academy)
resources.whyHelpful: Focused video with step-by-step walkthrough of EV calculation. Great for seeing the formula applied with concrete numbers before tackling harder problems.

resources.title: Risk and Return (Investopedia)
resources.whyHelpful: Practical introduction to the risk-return tradeoff in investing. Explains why higher expected returns require taking on more risk.

resources.title: Expected Value in Trading (The Balance)
resources.whyHelpful: Applies EV calculations directly to trading decisions. Shows how to compute the expected value of a single trade.

quiz.question: Coin flip: win $10 on heads, lose $8 on tails. What's the expected value?
quiz.explanation: EV = 0.5 × $10 + 0.5 × (-$8) = $5 - $4 = $1. This is a positive EV bet.

quiz.question: A strategy wins 40% of trades with avg win $300 and avg loss $150. What's the EV per trade?
quiz.explanation: EV = 0.4 × $300 + 0.6 × (-$150) = $120 - $90 = $30. Positive EV even with sub-50% win rate.

quiz.question: A game costs $5 to play. You have a 1% chance to win $400. Should you play?
options: Only if you can play many times
quiz.explanation: EV = 0.01 × $400 - $5 = $4 - $5 = -$1. Negative EV means you lose money on average.

quiz.question: After 100 trades of a +EV strategy, you should expect to be...
quiz.explanation: The law of large numbers means you'll converge toward the EV over time, but 100 trades still has significant variance. You're probably up, but not guaranteed.

workedExamples.problem: You are offered a bet: roll a fair die. If it lands 1 or 2, you lose $10. If it lands 3, 4, 5, or 6, you win $8. What is the expected value? Should you take this bet?
workedExamples.solution: P(win) = 4/6 = 2/3, P(lose) = 2/6 = 1/3. EV = (2/3 × $8) + (1/3 × -$10) = $5.33 - $3.33 = $2.00. This is a positive EV bet. You should take it if you can tolerate the variance and repeat it many times.
workedExamples.explanation: Positive expected value means you profit on average over many trials. But one trial still has a 33% chance of losing. EV guides long-term decisions, not single outcomes.

workedExamples.problem: You trade ETH perp futures with 10x leverage. Your $2k Coinbase account lets you open a $20k position. If ETH moves 5% against you, you're liquidated (lose 50% of account). If it moves 5% in your favor, you gain 50%. Your win rate is 40%. What is the EV of one trade?
workedExamples.solution: Win: +50% of $2k = +$1000. Lose: -50% of $2k = -$1000. EV = (0.4 × $1000) + (0.6 × -$1000) = $400 - $600 = -$200. This is a negative EV trade. Even with 10x leverage, a 40% win rate and 1:1 risk/reward loses money long-term.
workedExamples.explanation: Leverage amplifies both gains and losses. High leverage with low win rate is a recipe for ruin. This is why most perp traders lose money — negative EV disguised by exciting leverage.

workedExamples.problem: You bet $100 on a Polymarket prediction market. The market says 'ETH will hit $3000 by end of month' at 30% probability (0.30 price). You think the real probability is 50%. What is the EV of this bet?
workedExamples.solution: If you're right (50% probability): EV = (0.5 × $100/0.30 - $100) = (0.5 × $333.33 - $100) = $166.67 - $100 = $66.67. If the market is right (30%): EV = (0.3 × $333.33 - $100) = $100 - $100 = $0. Since you believe 50%, the EV is $66.67 per $100 bet. This is a +EV bet if your probability estimate is accurate.
workedExamples.explanation: Prediction markets let you bet on beliefs. EV = P(win) × (payout - cost) - P(lose) × cost. The key is having a better probability estimate than the market price.

commonMistakes: Rejecting positive EV bets because of risk aversion — this is rational for one-off life decisions, but irrational for repeatable trading decisions.
Ignoring the frequency of opportunities — a +$1 EV bet you can take 1000 times is worth more than a +$100 EV bet you can take once.
Confusing expected value with median outcome — EV is the average, not the most likely single outcome.

nodes.name: Bayesian Thinking
nodes.description: Updating beliefs with evidence. Bayes' theorem, priors, posteriors, and the trader's edge.
nodes.whyItMatters: Trading is Bayesian. You start with a prior belief (the trend is up). You see new data (a breakout). You update. Traders who ignore base rates and new evidence equally are flying blind.
practiceProblems: P(market up | Fed cuts rates). Use Bayes' theorem given historical data.
A strategy wins 55% historically. After 10 losses in a row, what's your updated win probability?
Explain the base rate fallacy with a medical test example
Prior: coin is fair. Data: 8 heads in 10 flips. Updated belief?

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Bayes' Theorem
resources.whyHelpful: Best visual explanation of Bayes theorem — watch this first to build intuition for how probabilities update with new evidence before diving into the math.

resources.title: Bayesian Thinking for Traders
resources.whyHelpful: Applies Bayesian updating directly to trading: how to update your position beliefs when new price data arrives.

resources.title: Think Bayes (free book)
resources.whyHelpful: Free Python-based book on Bayesian statistics. Uses computation instead of pure math - perfect if you learn by coding.

workedExamples.problem: A disease affects 1% of the population. A test is 95% accurate: 95% of sick people test positive, and 95% of healthy people test negative. You test positive. What is the probability you actually have the disease?
workedExamples.solution: P(Disease|Positive) = P(Positive|Disease) × P(Disease) / P(Positive). P(Positive) = 0.95×0.01 + 0.05×0.99 = 0.0095 + 0.0495 = 0.059. P(Disease|Positive) = 0.0095 / 0.059 ≈ 16.1%.
workedExamples.explanation: This is the base rate fallacy. Even with a 95% accurate test, if the disease is rare, most positive results are false positives. Always incorporate the base rate into your probability updates.

workedExamples.problem: You have a trading strategy with 40% historical win rate (prior). After 10 trades, you've won 6 (60% recent win rate). Use Bayesian updating to calculate your updated win rate belief. Assume Beta(4,6) prior (alpha=wins+1, beta=losses+1).
workedExamples.solution: Prior: Beta(4,6) → 4-1=3 wins, 6-1=5 losses → 3/8 = 37.5% win rate. New data: 6 wins, 4 losses. Posterior: Beta(4+6, 6+4) = Beta(10,10) → 9/19 ≈ 47.4% win rate. The 10-trade sample nudged your belief from 37.5% to 47.4%.
workedExamples.explanation: Bayesian updating combines prior belief with new evidence. The Beta distribution is the conjugate prior for binomial outcomes (win/loss). This is how you adapt your strategy beliefs in real-time.

workedExamples.problem: Write Python code to update a Bayesian win rate estimate after each trade. Start with 40% prior, update after a win or loss.
workedExamples.explanation: This Python class implements Bayesian updating for win rate. Pseudocounts (4,6) represent your prior belief. Each trade updates the posterior. This is how professional quants track strategy performance.

workedExamples.problem: You believe a new trading strategy has a 55% win rate (prior). After 20 trades, you observe 8 wins (40% observed rate). Should you abandon the strategy? Update your belief using a Beta(55,45) prior (representing 55% confidence with 100 pseudo-observations), then calculate the probability the true win rate exceeds 50%.
workedExamples.solution: Prior: Beta(55,45). New data: 8 wins, 12 losses. Posterior: Beta(55+8, 45+12) = Beta(63,57). Posterior mean = 63/120 = 52.5%. Probability win rate > 50%: Using Beta CDF, P(θ > 0.5 | data) ≈ 60%. The posterior has shifted downward but still slightly favors positive edge. Verdict: don't abandon yet, but collect more data. 20 trades is too small to overturn a moderate prior.
workedExamples.explanation: Bayesian updating prevents overreacting to small samples. The prior represents your accumulated knowledge; 20 trades shouldn't flip your entire belief. But if after 100 more trades the rate stays at 40%, the data will overwhelm the prior. This is how rational traders balance skepticism with open-mindedness.

commonMistakes: Ignoring the prior (base rate) — the probability of a hypothesis before seeing evidence is often the most important factor.
Treating P(A|B) and P(B|A) as equal — they are only equal when P(A) = P(B), which is rare.
Updating beliefs too strongly from weak evidence — small samples should cause small updates.

quiz.question: In Bayes' Theorem, what does the prior represent?
options: The new evidence you just observed
Your belief before seeing the evidence
The probability of the evidence given any hypothesis
quiz.explanation: The prior P(H) represents your initial belief about a hypothesis before considering the new evidence.

quiz.question: A strategy has historically won 60% of the time. After 3 losses in a row, a Bayesian trader would...
options: Conclude the strategy is broken and stop using it
Update their belief slightly downward but still expect positive edge
Double their bet size to recover losses
Assume the next trade must be a win
quiz.explanation: Bayesian updating incorporates new evidence gradually. Three losses slightly reduce the estimated win rate but don't overturn a strong prior built on hundreds of trades.

quiz.question: What is the base rate fallacy?
options: Ignoring the prior probability when interpreting new evidence
Confusing Type I and Type II errors
quiz.explanation: The base rate fallacy happens when you ignore the underlying base rate (prior) and focus only on new evidence. It's why a 95% accurate test still yields mostly false positives for rare diseases.

nodes.name: Correlation & Regression
nodes.description: Measuring relationships between variables. Correlation, linear regression, and R².
nodes.whyItMatters: Does Bitcoin follow the S&P 500? Is there a relationship between volume and volatility? Correlation and regression answer these. But beware — correlation without causation is how amateurs get rekt.
practiceProblems: Calculate correlation between two small datasets by hand
Given regression line y = 2x + 3, predict y when x = 5
R² = 0.85. What does that mean?
Find a spurious correlation online and explain why it's not causal

resources.title: Khan Academy: Correlation
resources.whyHelpful: Structured course on correlation, regression, and scatter plots. Interactive practice problems with instant feedback.

resources.title: Correlation vs Causation
resources.whyHelpful: Explains why correlated variables are often both caused by a third factor. Critical lesson for avoiding spurious trading signals.

resources.title: Spurious Correlations Website
resources.whyHelpful: Fun examples of absurdly high correlations with zero causation. Memorably demonstrates why correlation does not equal causation.

workedExamples.problem: You collect daily BTC returns and S&P 500 returns for 30 days. The correlation coefficient is r = 0.35. You fit a regression: BTC_return = 0.8 × SPX_return + 0.2. If S&P rises 1% tomorrow, what does the model predict for BTC? How much of BTC's variance is explained by SPX?
workedExamples.solution: Predicted BTC return = 0.8 × 1% + 0.2% = 1.0%. R² = r² = 0.35² = 0.1225. About 12.25% of BTC's variance is explained by S&P 500 movements in this model.
workedExamples.explanation: Correlation measures linear relationship strength (-1 to +1). Regression predicts one variable from another. R² tells you the proportion of variance explained — most of BTC's movement is driven by other factors.

workedExamples.problem: You analyze 60 days of ETH and SOL returns. The correlation is r = 0.72. However, you suspect this is driven by both assets responding to BTC movements. How would you test for this? Calculate the partial correlation between ETH and SOL controlling for BTC.
workedExamples.solution: First, regress ETH on BTC to get residuals (ETH movement not explained by BTC). Then regress SOL on BTC to get residuals. The correlation between these two residuals is the partial correlation. If partial r drops from 0.72 to 0.20, most of the ETH-SOL correlation was actually both following BTC. If it stays at 0.70, ETH and SOL have genuine co-movement beyond BTC.
workedExamples.explanation: Spurious correlation is a major trap in trading analysis. Two assets may appear correlated simply because they both follow a third variable (like BTC). Partial correlation controls for confounding variables. This is essential for building truly diversified portfolios.

workedExamples.problem: You fit a linear regression of stock returns vs market returns: Return = 0.02 + 1.3 × MarketReturn. Over 100 days, you get R² = 0.72. Looking at the residuals, you notice three data points are 4 standard deviations away from the regression line. A new trader claims 'the model predicts well since R² = 0.72.' How do you assess this claim?
workedExamples.solution: R² = 0.72 means 72% of variance is explained — reasonably good. But 3 outliers at 4σ (where you'd expect ~0 in a normal distribution) indicates the model is misspecified or the data has fat tails. These extreme residuals heavily influence the regression coefficients. Remove or investigate the outliers, then re-fit. If R² drops to 0.45, the original 0.72 was inflated by extreme points. Residual analysis is essential — a high R² with patterned residuals means the model is capturing the wrong relationships.
workedExamples.explanation: Residuals reveal what R² hides: outliers, nonlinearity, heteroskedasticity. Always plot residuals after fitting a regression. Three 4σ outliers in 100 points strongly suggests a heavy-tailed distribution or structural breaks. In finance, normal assumptions about residuals almost always fail — fat tails are the rule, not the exception.

commonMistakes: Confusing correlation with causation — just because two assets move together doesn't mean one causes the other.
Using r = 0.35 to claim a strong relationship — correlations below 0.5 explain less than 25% of variance.
Extrapolating regression beyond the data range — relationships that hold in normal conditions may break during crises.

quiz.question: If correlation between X and Y is -0.8, what does this mean?
options: When X increases, Y tends to decrease strongly
quiz.explanation: A strong negative correlation (-0.8) means that as X increases, Y tends to decrease in a roughly linear fashion. Correlation does not imply causation.

quiz.question: In regression, what does R² = 0.64 mean?
options: 64% of the variance in the dependent variable is explained by the model
quiz.explanation: R² (coefficient of determination) represents the proportion of variance in the dependent variable that is predictable from the independent variable(s).

quiz.question: What is a spurious correlation?
options: A correlation that is statistically significant
A correlation that appears real but is caused by a third variable or coincidence
quiz.explanation: Spurious correlations are coincidental or driven by a confounding variable. Example: ice cream sales correlate with drowning deaths (both rise in summer). They are not causal.

nodes.name: Hypothesis Testing
nodes.description: Null hypotheses, p-values, confidence intervals, Type I and Type II errors.
keyConcepts: Null hypothesis H₀ and alternative H₁
nodes.whyItMatters: Does your new strategy actually work, or did you get lucky? Hypothesis testing separates signal from noise. Without it, you're just pattern-matching on randomness.
practiceProblems: You test a strategy on 50 trades, win rate 62%. Is it significantly better than 50%?
What's the difference between a p-value of 0.05 and 0.01?
You run 20 backtests. One shows p < 0.05. Is it valid?
Explain Type I vs Type II error with a trading example

resources.title: Khan Academy: Hypothesis Testing
resources.whyHelpful: Step-by-step walkthrough of hypothesis testing with practice problems. Covers null hypotheses, p-values, and significance levels.

resources.title: p-values Explained
resources.whyHelpful: Clear explanation of what p-values do and do not mean. Helps avoid the most common misinterpretation in trading backtests.

resources.title: StatQuest: Hypothesis Testing
resources.whyHelpful: Josh Starmer's clear, step-by-step breakdown of null hypotheses, p-values, and Type I/II errors. StatQuest makes statistics intuitive and memorable.

workedExamples.problem: You claim your new trading strategy has a win rate above 50%. You test it on 100 trades and observe 58 wins. Using a significance level of α = 0.05, do you reject the null hypothesis?
workedExamples.solution: H₀: p = 0.50. Sample proportion = 0.58. Standard error = √(0.5×0.5/100) = 0.05. Z = (0.58 - 0.50) / 0.05 = 1.6. Two-tailed p-value ≈ 0.11. Since 0.11 > 0.05, you fail to reject H₀. The result is not statistically significant at the 5% level.
workedExamples.explanation: A p-value of 0.11 means: if the true win rate were 50%, there's an 11% chance of seeing 58+ wins by random chance. This is too high to confidently claim edge. You need more trades or a larger effect.

workedExamples.problem: You backtest 50 different strategy variations and find one with a Sharpe ratio of 2.5 on 200 trades (avg strategies have Sharpe 0.5). Is this result statistically significant, or could it be from multiple testing? Apply the Bonferroni correction.
workedExamples.solution: Without correction: Z = (2.5 - 0.5)/SE. With 50 tests, Bonferroni adjusts α from 0.05 to 0.05/50 = 0.001. The critical Z for α=0.001 is 3.29 instead of 1.96. Your strategy needs Z > 3.29 to be significant after correction. If SE ≈ 0.3, Z = (2.5-0.5)/0.3 = 6.67, which exceeds 3.29. The result survives Bonferroni correction.
workedExamples.explanation: Multiple testing is a silent killer of strategy research. Test 50 strategies and one will look great by chance. Bonferroni correction divides α by the number of tests. This is why out-of-sample testing and walk-forward analysis are essential in quantitative trading.

workedExamples.problem: You run an A/B test on your Polymarket predictions. In 150 predictions with a structured method, you win 88 (58.7%). Your baseline gut-feel method had 48% over a larger sample. Is your structured method significantly better at the alpha = 0.01 level? What does this mean practically for your betting size?
workedExamples.solution: H_sub_0: p = 0.48. Sample proportion = 0.587. SE = sqrt(0.48*0.52/150) = sqrt(0.001664) = 0.0408. Z = (0.587 - 0.48) / 0.0408 = 2.62. Critical Z for alpha = 0.01 (one-tailed) is 2.326. Since 2.62 > 2.326, we reject H_sub_0 at the 1% level. The p-value approx 0.0044. With 99% confidence, the structured method outperforms gut feel. Practical impact: you can now size your bets larger using the structured method, knowing the edge is real and not a fluke.
workedExamples.explanation: A/B testing is everywhere in prediction markets and trading. A p-value of 0.0044 means there is only a 0.44% chance of seeing this result if both methods were equal. Using a stricter alpha = 0.01 (instead of 0.05) reduces false discoveries when you are going to increase position size based on the conclusion. Always decide your significance level BEFORE looking at the data - this avoids p-hacking.

commonMistakes: Treating p < 0.05 as proof the strategy works — it only means the observed result is unlikely under the null hypothesis, not that your alternative is true.
Running multiple tests and only reporting significant ones — this is p-hacking and produces false positives.
Using a one-tailed test when you should use two-tailed — if you care about deviation in either direction, use two-tailed.

quiz.question: What does a p-value of 0.03 mean?
options: There is a 3% probability that the null hypothesis is true
If the null hypothesis were true, there is a 3% chance of observing data this extreme
The alternative hypothesis has a 97% chance of being true
quiz.explanation: The p-value is the probability of observing the data (or more extreme) assuming the null hypothesis is true. It does NOT give the probability that the null is true.

quiz.question: What is a Type I error?
options: Failing to detect a true effect
Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true
quiz.explanation: A Type I error (false positive) occurs when you conclude there is an effect when there isn't one. The significance level α controls the Type I error rate.

quiz.question: What does a 95% confidence interval for a mean tell you?
options: There is a 95% chance the true mean is inside the interval
If you repeated the experiment many times, 95% of the intervals would contain the true mean
The sample mean has a 95% chance of being correct
95% of the data falls within the interval
quiz.explanation: A 95% confidence interval means that if you repeated the sampling process many times, 95% of the intervals constructed would contain the true population mean. It does NOT give the probability that the true mean is in this specific interval.

nodes.name: Sampling & Central Limit Theorem
nodes.description: How sample statistics approximate population parameters. The CLT and standard error.
nodes.whyItMatters: You can't run infinite backtests. You sample. The CLT tells you that with enough samples, your estimate converges to truth. This is why 30+ trades matter more than 5.
practiceProblems: Population std dev = 10. Sample size = 25. Standard error?
Why does CLT apply to sample means but not necessarily individual values?
How many trades needed for 95% CI within ±5% of true win rate?
Explain why larger samples reduce uncertainty

resources.title: Khan Academy: CLT
resources.whyHelpful: Covers sampling distributions, standard error, and the CLT with practice exercises. Foundation for understanding how sample means converge to population means.

resources.title: CLT Visualized
resources.whyHelpful: Animated demonstration of how sample means from any distribution converge to normal. Builds intuition that equations alone cannot.

resources.title: StatQuest: Central Limit Theorem
resources.whyHelpful: StatQuest clear explanation of why sample means converge to a normal distribution. The visual examples make the CLT click in a way that formulas alone cannot.

workedExamples.problem: You sample 100 trades from a strategy with a true win rate of 55%. What is the standard error of the sample proportion? If you increase the sample to 400 trades, how does the standard error change?
workedExamples.explanation: Standard error decreases with the square root of sample size. To cut uncertainty in half, you need 4x the data. This is why small sample sizes give unreliable estimates of strategy performance.

workedExamples.problem: You want to estimate your strategy's true win rate within ±3% with 95% confidence. Your estimated win rate is 45%. How many trades do you need? What if you want ±1% precision?
workedExamples.explanation: Sample size planning is crucial before evaluating a strategy. To double the precision, you need 4x the data. Many traders evaluate strategies on too few trades and draw false conclusions. Plan your sample size before you start collecting data.

workedExamples.problem: You have a dataset of 50 trade PnLs: [-120, +85, +210, -45, +330, -90, +150, -200, +60, +110, -30, +420, -75, +95, -160, +50, +280, -55, +190, -140, +75, -110, +500, -25, +95, -85, +600, -300, +45, +120, -70, +180, -15, +250, -40, +320, -210, +85, +160, -95, +55, -65, +390, -50, +430, -180, +110, +70, +140, -35]. Use bootstrapping to estimate the 95% confidence interval of the average PnL without assuming normality. Compare this to the parametric CI assuming returns are normal.
workedExamples.solution: First, calculate sample statistics: mean = $84.80, std dev = $199.72, n = 50. Parametric 95% CI: mean ± 1.96 × std/√n = 84.80 ± 1.96 × 199.72/√50 = 84.80 ± 55.37 = [$29.43, $140.17]. For bootstrap CI: resample with replacement 10,000 times, compute each mean, sort, find 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles. Bootstrapped 95% CI: [$40.20, $138.50]. The bootstrap CI is narrower and shifted right because real trade PnL distributions have positive skew (big winners stretch the right tail), violating normality assumptions. The parametric CI assumes symmetry and gives a misleading lower bound that's too low.
workedExamples.explanation: Bootstrapping is a non-parametric method that resamples from your actual data — no normality assumption needed. When trade PnLs have skew (most small losses, few large wins), the parametric CI underestimates the true lower bound and overestimates uncertainty. Always bootstrap when you have <100 trades and returns look non-normal. For this 50-trade sample, your estimate is that the true average PnL is between $40 and $138 per trade with 95% confidence.

commonMistakes: Assuming 30 trades is enough to evaluate a strategy — 30 is a rule of thumb for CLT, not for precise estimation of win rates.
Ignoring that SE depends on both sample size and the true proportion — SE is largest when p = 0.5.
Confusing standard deviation (population spread) with standard error (uncertainty of the sample mean).

quiz.question: What does the Central Limit Theorem state?
options: Sample means approach a normal distribution as sample size increases, regardless of the population distribution
The population mean equals the sample mean
Standard deviation decreases with sample size
quiz.explanation: The CLT states that the sampling distribution of the mean approaches a normal distribution as the sample size grows, even if the original population is not normally distributed.

quiz.question: If you quadruple your sample size, what happens to the standard error of the mean?
quiz.explanation: Standard error = σ/√n. If n becomes 4n, SE becomes σ/√(4n) = (σ/√n)/2. The standard error is halved.

quiz.question: What is standard error?
options: The standard deviation of the population
The standard deviation of the sample mean's sampling distribution
The error in the sample mean
quiz.explanation: Standard error (SE) measures the variability of the sample mean across repeated samples. SE = σ/√n. It decreases as sample size increases.

quiz.question: What is the minimum sample size for the CLT to apply?
quiz.explanation: While there's no universal minimum, n = 30 is a common rule of thumb for the CLT to approximate normality. For skewed populations, you may need more.

quiz.question: A trader samples 100 trades and finds a 45% win rate. What does the law of large numbers say?
options: The true win rate must be 45%
With more trades, the sample win rate will converge to the true win rate
The sample win rate will become exactly 50%
The sample size is too small to conclude anything
quiz.explanation: The law of large numbers states that as sample size increases, the sample mean (win rate) converges to the true population mean. 100 trades is a decent start but more trades reduce uncertainty.

nodes.name: Monte Carlo Simulation
nodes.description: Using randomness to solve complex problems. Bootstrapping, path simulation, and risk modeling.
nodes.whyItMatters: Monte Carlo lets you simulate 10,000 trading years in seconds. You can estimate worst-case drawdowns, optimal position sizes, and the probability of ruin. This is how quants think about risk.
practiceProblems: Simulate 1000 sequences of 100 trades each. What's the max drawdown distribution?
Given historical returns, bootstrap a 95% CI for annual return
What's the probability of ruin with 2% risk per trade and 35% win rate?
How does Monte Carlo differ from historical backtesting?

resources.title: Monte Carlo Simulation Explained
resources.whyHelpful: Clear introduction to Monte Carlo methods with practical examples. Shows how random sampling solves problems that are analytically intractable.

resources.title: Bootstrapping in Statistics
resources.whyHelpful: Explains how resampling with replacement estimates uncertainty without distributional assumptions. Essential for trading strategies with non-normal returns.

resources.title: Monte Carlo Simulation (3Blue1Brown)
resources.whyHelpful: 3Blue1Brown elegant visual demo of Monte Carlo methods. Shows how randomness can solve deterministic problems like estimating π — builds intuition for the power of simulation.

workedExamples.problem: A trading strategy has a 40% win rate, average win of $200, and average loss of $100. You risk 2% per trade on a $10,000 account. Simulate 1000 paths of 100 trades each using Monte Carlo. What metrics would you report?
workedExamples.solution: For each of 1000 simulations, generate 100 trades (win/loss based on 40% probability), apply position sizing, and track final equity. Report: median final equity, 5th percentile (worst case), 95th percentile (best case), max drawdown distribution, and probability of ruin (account < 50% of start).
workedExamples.explanation: Monte Carlo turns one historical backtest into a distribution of possible futures. It reveals tail risks that a single backtest hides. The 5th percentile tells you what 'bad luck' looks like.

workedExamples.problem: Write a Python function to bootstrap the 95% confidence interval for a strategy's average trade PnL. Given an array of 200 trade PnLs, resample with replacement 10,000 times and find the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of the mean.
workedExamples.solution:
import numpy as np
def bootstrap_ci(trades, n_bootstrap=10000, ci=0.95):
    means = []
    for _ in range(n_bootstrap):
        sample = np.random.choice(trades, size=len(trades), replace=True)
        means.append(np.mean(sample))
    lower = np.percentile(means, (1-ci)/2 * 100)
    upper = np.percentile(means, (1+ci)/2 * 100)
    return lower, upper

trades = np.random.normal(50, 200, 200)  # avg $50, std $200
lower, upper = bootstrap_ci(trades)
print(f"95% CI for avg PnL: [{lower:.2f}, {upper:.2f}]")
workedExamples.explanation: Bootstrapping makes no assumptions about the distribution of returns. It resamples your actual data to estimate uncertainty. This is more robust than parametric methods when returns are not normally distributed (which they almost never are in trading).

workedExamples.problem: You have a $10,000 trading account, a strategy with 40% win rate, risk 2% per trade ($200), and average win of $500, average loss of $200. Use Monte Carlo simulation to estimate the probability of ruin (account drops below $5,000) over 500 trades. Run 10,000 simulations.
workedExamples.solution: For each simulation: initialize equity = 10,000. For 500 trades: generate a uniform random u. If u < 0.40 (win), equity += $500; else equity -= $200. If equity < 5,000 at any point, mark as ruined. After 10,000 simulations, ruin_probability = ruined_count / 10,000. Expected result: ~8-12% ruin probability. With 2% risk per trade and 40% win rate, the expected return per trade is 0.40x500 + 0.60x(-200) = $80. But variance from losing streaks causes ruin. Reducing risk to 1% ($100 loss, ~$250 win) drops ruin probability below 1%.
workedExamples.explanation: Ruin probability is the single most important risk metric for a trader. It captures what average trade statistics hide - the possibility of a string of losses that wipes you out. Monte Carlo is the only practical way to estimate it, since the exact formula depends on the sequence of wins and losses which is inherently random. The key insight: even a profitable strategy can ruin you if you risk too much per trade. This simulation shows why professional traders use fractional Kelly sizing and never risk more than 1-2% per trade.

commonMistakes: Assuming returns are independent when they have autocorrelation — if losses cluster, Monte Carlo with independent sampling underestimates tail risk.
Using normal distributions for returns when actual returns have fat tails — this understates the probability of extreme moves.
Running too few simulations — 100 paths is noisy; 10,000+ gives stable estimates of percentiles.

quiz.question: What is the primary purpose of Monte Carlo simulation in trading?
options: To model the distribution of possible outcomes and estimate tail risks
quiz.explanation: Monte Carlo simulation generates thousands of possible future paths to estimate the distribution of outcomes, including worst-case scenarios and drawdowns.

quiz.question: Bootstrapping in statistics means...
options: Starting a business with no money
Resampling with replacement from observed data to estimate uncertainty
Using only the best trades in analysis
quiz.explanation: Bootstrapping resamples the existing data with replacement to create new samples, which helps estimate confidence intervals without assuming a specific distribution.

quiz.question: What does a 95% 1-day VaR of $1000 mean?
options: You will lose exactly $1000 in 5% of days
There is a 5% chance of losing $1000 or more in a day
You will lose at least $1000 in 95% of days
quiz.explanation: Value at Risk (VaR) at 95% confidence means that in 95% of cases, the loss will be less than $1000; in 5% of cases, the loss will be $1000 or more.

nodes.name: Statistics for Traders
nodes.description: Sharpe ratio, max drawdown, win rate significance, Kelly criterion, and portfolio metrics.
nodes.whyItMatters: These are the numbers that separate pros from gamblers. A trader with a 1.5 Sharpe ratio and controlled drawdown is investable. A trader with random metrics is just gambling with extra steps.
practiceProblems: Calculate Sharpe ratio: avg daily return 0.05%, std dev 0.8%, risk-free 0.01%
Kelly fraction given 55% win rate and 2:1 payoff. Bet size?
Profit factor = gross profit / gross loss. What's a good value?
Compare two strategies: A has higher return, B has higher Sharpe. Which is better?

resources.title: Portfolio Metrics (Investopedia)
resources.whyHelpful: Reference for Sharpe ratio, Sortino ratio, and other performance metrics. Clear formulas and worked examples you can apply to your own trading data.

resources.title: Sharpe Ratio Explained (Khan Academy)
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy approach to risk-adjusted returns. Builds the math from first principles so you understand what the ratio actually measures.

resources.title: Kelly Criterion Deep Dive
resources.whyHelpful: Optimal bet sizing derived from first principles. Explains why full Kelly maximizes long-term growth but most professionals use fractional Kelly.

resources.title: Maximum Drawdown Explained
resources.whyHelpful: Defines max drawdown, recovery time, and why it matters more than total return. Realistic assessment of the worst-case scenario for any strategy.

workedExamples.problem: Strategy A: avg daily return 0.08%, std dev 0.9%, 200 trades. Strategy B: avg daily return 0.12%, std dev 1.8%, 200 trades. Risk-free rate is 0.01%. Calculate Sharpe ratios. Which is better?
workedExamples.solution: Sharpe A = (0.08 - 0.01) / 0.9 = 0.0778. Sharpe B = (0.12 - 0.01) / 1.8 = 0.0611. Strategy A has a higher risk-adjusted return. Even though B makes more nominally, it takes twice the risk per unit of return. Kelly fraction for A: (0.08/0.9²) ≈ 9.9% per trade (theoretical max).
workedExamples.explanation: Sharpe ratio measures return per unit of risk. Higher is better. Raw return without considering volatility is misleading. The Kelly criterion tells you the theoretical optimal bet size for a given edge and variance.

workedExamples.problem: Your strategy has a 55% win rate with average win $300 and average loss $200. Calculate the Kelly criterion optimal position size as a percentage of your $50,000 account. Then calculate half-Kelly for safety. What is the expected growth rate at full Kelly vs half Kelly?
workedExamples.solution: Kelly f* = (p × b - q) / b where b = avg_win/avg_loss = 300/200 = 1.5, p = 0.55, q = 0.45. f* = (0.55 × 1.5 - 0.45) / 1.5 = (0.825 - 0.45) / 1.5 = 0.25 = 25%. Half Kelly = 12.5%. Bet size: 12.5% of $50,000 = $6,250 per trade. Expected log growth at full Kelly is maximized but volatile. Half Kelly gives ~75% of the growth rate with much less drawdown risk.
workedExamples.explanation: Full Kelly maximizes long-term growth but produces extreme drawdowns. Most professional traders use half-Kelly or quarter-Kelly to account for estimation error in their edge. The difference in expected growth is small, but the difference in drawdown risk is enormous.

workedExamples.problem: Your trading strategy achieved a Sharpe ratio of 0.8 over 252 daily returns, with return skewness of −0.5 and excess kurtosis of 3. Is this Sharpe ratio statistically significant at the 95% confidence level? Use Opdyke's formula for the standard error of the Sharpe ratio: SE(SR) = √[(1 + 0.5 × SR² − γ₃ × SR + (γ₄ − 3)/4 × SR²) / n] where γ₃ is skewness, γ₄ is excess kurtosis, and n is the number of returns. Construct the 95% confidence interval.
workedExamples.solution:
Given: SR = 0.8, n = 252, γ₃ = −0.5, γ₄ = 3 (excess kurtosis).

SE(SR) = √[(1 + 0.5 × 0.8² − (−0.5) × 0.8 + (3 − 3)/4 × 0.8²) / 252]
      = √[(1 + 0.32 + 0.40 + 0) / 252] = √[1.72 / 252] = √0.006825 = 0.0826

95% CI: SR ± 1.96 × SE(SR) = 0.8 ± 1.96 × 0.0826 = 0.8 ± 0.162
95% CI: [0.638, 0.962]

The lower bound is 0.638, well above 0. The Sharpe ratio is statistically significant at 95% confidence. Z-score = 0.8 / 0.0826 ≈ 9.68, p-value ≈ 0.

If the Sharpe were 0.3: SE = √[(1 + 0.5 × 0.09 + 0.15 + 0) / 252] = √[1.195/252] = 0.0689. 95% CI: [0.165, 0.435]. Still significant if data is clean. But with only 63 days (quarter): SE = √[1.195/63] = 0.138. 95% CI: [0.030, 0.571] — barely significant. Always demand 1+ year of data.
workedExamples.explanation: The standard error of the Sharpe ratio increases with negative skew (more downside tail risk) and high kurtosis (more extreme outcomes). A Sharpe of 0.5 with 6 months of data may not be significant, but the same Sharpe with 3 years of data is. This is why professional funds require 3+ years of audited returns before considering a strategy investable. Always report the confidence interval, not just the point estimate.

commonMistakes: Comparing strategies by total return alone — a 100% return with 50% drawdown is worse than 50% return with 10% drawdown.
Using full Kelly in practice — half Kelly or quarter Kelly is safer because it accounts for estimation error in your edge.
Ignoring that Sharpe ratio assumes normally distributed returns — for fat-tailed strategies, Sortino ratio (downside deviation only) is more informative.

quiz.question: What does the Sharpe ratio measure?
options: Return per unit of total risk (standard deviation)
quiz.explanation: Sharpe ratio = (Return - Risk-free rate) / Standard Deviation. It measures risk-adjusted return — how much excess return you earn per unit of volatility.

quiz.question: The Kelly criterion tells you...
options: The optimal position size to maximize long-term growth
The probability of winning the next trade
quiz.explanation: Kelly criterion calculates the fraction of your bankroll to bet to maximize expected logarithmic wealth. Most traders use fractional Kelly (half or quarter) for safety.

quiz.question: What does maximum drawdown measure?
options: Largest peak-to-trough decline in account value
quiz.explanation: Max drawdown is the largest percentage drop from a peak to a subsequent trough. It measures the worst-case risk a trader has experienced.

nodes.name: Experimental Design & A/B Testing
nodes.description: Design experiments that produce trustworthy conclusions. Sample size calculation, randomization, A/B testing, statistical vs practical significance, multiple testing correction, and pitfalls that invalidate experiments.
keyConcepts: Type I error (α) and Type II error (β)
Sample size calculation for A/B tests
Peeking problem: why stopping early invalidates results
nodes.whyItMatters: Bad experimental design is the #1 source of false discoveries in science, trading, and business. Every 'promising' trading signal needs rigorous A/B testing before you risk real money. Understanding experimental design separates rigorous thinkers from pattern-matchers who see meaning in noise.
practiceProblems: You run an A/B test: 1000 visitors per variant, conversion rate 5.2% vs 6.8%. Is this statistically significant at α=0.05? Calculate the p-value.
Your trading bot's A/B test shows +12% return vs baseline. Only 30 trades per variant. Why might this result be unreliable?

At your current signal detection threshold, 5% of tests produce false positives. If you test 20 trading signals per month, how many false positives do you expect? What if you use Bonferroni correction?
An experiment has 40% power to detect a 2% effect. If the true effect is 2%, what's the probability of missing it? How many samples would you need for 80% power?

resources.title: A/B Testing Guide (Optimizely)
resources.whyHelpful: Practical guide to A/B testing from industry pioneers. Covers sample size, duration, and common pitfalls with real examples from e-commerce and SaaS.

resources.title: Statistical Power Explained (StatQuest)
resources.whyHelpful: StatQuest's clear animated explanation of statistical power. Makes a tricky concept intuitive — understanding why underpowered studies waste time.

resources.title: Multiple Testing Problem (Simply Psychology)
resources.whyHelpful: Clear explanation of Bonferroni correction and why multiple comparisons inflate false positive rates. Essential for avoiding 'finding patterns in noise.'

resources.title: Sample Size Calculator (Evan's Awesome)
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive sample size calculator by Evan Miller. Plug in your baseline rate and minimum detectable effect — instantly see required sample size for 80% power.

workedExamples.problem: You run an A/B test on a trading signal: 100 trades with the signal (variant) vs 100 without (control). Control: avg return +$2.00, std $15.00. Variant: avg return +$5.50, std $17.00. Is this difference statistically significant at α=0.05? What sample size would give you 80% power to detect the observed effect size?
workedExamples.solution:
Step 1: Calculate the test statistic.
  SE = √[ ($15²/100) + ($17²/100) ] = √[2.25 + 2.89] = √5.14 = $2.27
  t = ($5.50 - $2.00) / $2.27 = $3.50 / $2.27 = 1.54
  df ≈ 198 (Welch-Satterthwaite), critical t ≈ 1.97 (two-tailed, α=0.05)
  |1.54| < 1.97 → NOT statistically significant
  p-value ≈ 0.125

Step 2: Observed effect size = $3.50 / (pooled std ≈ $16) = 0.219σ
  Required N for 80% power at α=0.05 (two-tailed) for effect 0.219σ:
  N ≈ (2 × (z_α/2 + z_β)²) / d² = (2 × (1.96 + 0.84)²) / 0.219²
  N ≈ (2 × 7.84) / 0.048 = 15.68 / 0.048 ≈ 327 per group
  Current N = 100 per group → severely underpowered

Conclusion: The $3.50/trade difference looks promising but is not statistically significant with only 100 trades. You'd need ~327 trades per group to detect it reliably. With 100 trades, the probability of missing a real effect of this size (Type II error) is ~60-70%.
workedExamples.explanation: Underpowered experiments are a massive problem — they waste time, produce misleading results, and fail to detect real effects. The rule: calculate required sample size BEFORE running the experiment, not after. If you can't get enough samples, either accept lower power, increase the effect size threshold, or don't run the test. For trading: 100 trades is rarely enough. Demand 300+ trades per variant for reliable A/B tests.

workedExamples.problem: You're testing 20 different technical trading indicators. For each indicator, you run an A/B test at α=0.05. Three indicators show p < 0.05. What's the probability that at least one of these is a false positive? Apply Bonferroni correction. What would the corrected threshold be?
workedExamples.solution:
Step 1: Expected false positives without correction.
  20 tests × 0.05 = 1 expected false positive
  Probability of at least one false positive: 1 - (0.95)²⁰ = 1 - 0.358 = 0.642 = 64.2%
  You have a 64% chance of finding at least one 'significant' result by chance alone.

Step 2: Bonferroni correction.
  Corrected α = 0.05 / 20 = 0.0025
  Only results with p < 0.0025 are significant.

Step 3: Re-evaluate the 3 'significant' indicators.
  Indicator A: p = 0.001 → still significant (0.001 < 0.0025) ✓
  Indicator B: p = 0.023 → NOT significant after correction ✗
  Indicator C: p = 0.009 → NOT significant after correction ✗

Only 1 of 3 'significant' results survives multiple testing correction. The other 2 are likely false positives — noise that looked like signal.

Alternative: False Discovery Rate (FDR) control: sort p-values, rank them, accept if p ≤ (rank/N) × Q where Q=0.05. FDR allows more discoveries than Bonferroni while still controlling the proportion of false positives.
workedExamples.explanation: The multiple testing problem is everywhere in trading: testing 20 indicators, 50 chart patterns, 10 timeframes. Without correction, you WILL find 'significant' patterns that are pure noise. Bonferroni is conservative but simple. FDR control (Benjamini-Hochberg) is preferred in modern practice because it has more power to find real effects while still controlling false discovery rate.

workedExamples.problem: You're A/B testing a new trading bot. After 3 days (15 trades), the variant is up 8% vs control's 2%. The p-value is 0.04. You stop the test and declare success. What's wrong? Calculate the actual false positive rate given that you peeked 3 times (after 15, 30, and 45 trades) using sequential testing correction.
workedExamples.solution:
The problem is peeking: checking results early and stopping when significant. With 3 peeks: actual α ≈ 0.05 × 3 = 0.15 (rough approximation, true value depends on correlation). More precisely: for sequential testing with O'Brien-Fleming spending: at 3 interim looks, spend α ≈ 0.005 for the first look, 0.015 for second, 0.030 for final.

Your peek after 15 trades: p=0.04 > 0.005 → should NOT have stopped

Solution: Choose one of:
1. Fixed horizon: Decide sample size upfront. Don't peek at all.
2. Sequential testing (Pocock/O'Brien-Fleming): Set stopping rules BEFORE starting. Use adjusted boundaries.
3. Bayesian approach: Update posterior after each batch, stop when 95% credible interval excludes zero.

For your $2k account: fixed horizon is simplest. Decide: 'I will run this test for 60 trades, analyze at the end.' No peeking. If you must peek, use the O'Brien-Fleming spending function — it adjusts thresholds to maintain overall Type I error rate.
workedExamples.explanation: Peeking is the most common A/B testing mistake in trading. You see early promising results, get excited, stop the test early, and deploy a strategy that doesn't work. The false positive rate from peeking can be 3-5x higher than nominal. If your test 'looks great' after 1 week, that's suspicious — not a signal to stop. Fix: pre-register your test duration. For individual traders: use fixed horizon with no peeking. Your time horizon is long — waiting 60 trades (1-2 months of signals) is nothing compared to a lifetime of trading.

commonMistakes: Stopping the experiment early when results look promising — this inflates false positive rates by 3-5x.
Running tests with too few samples (underpowered) — you can't prove a real effect exists even if it does. Most trading strategies need 300+ trades per variant.
Testing too many variants/indicators without multiple testing correction — with 20 indicators, you expect 1 false positive by chance at α=0.05.
Confusing 'statistically significant' with 'practically significant' — a $0.50/trade advantage may be 'real' but not worth deploying.
Ignoring regression to the mean — a hot trading strategy will likely cool down in live trading.
Not pre-registering the experiment design — it's easy to p-hack when you analyze before deciding what to measure.

quiz.question: An A/B test has 200 trades per variant. The observed difference is $2.50/trade with p=0.08. What's the main issue?
options: The difference is too small to matter
The test may be underpowered — not enough samples to detect a real effect
A/B testing doesn't work for trading
quiz.explanation: With p=0.08 > α=0.05, the result is NOT statistically significant. But with only 200 trades, the test likely has low power — meaning a real effect might exist but you can't detect it. The answer is NOT to deploy the strategy, but to run a larger test. Calculate required sample size before testing.

quiz.question: You test 15 trading indicators at α=0.05. Using Bonferroni correction, what's your new significance threshold?
quiz.explanation: Bonferroni correction divides α by the number of tests: 0.05 / 15 = 0.00333. Only indicators with p < 0.0033 are considered significant. This is conservative but protects against false positives from multiple comparisons.

quiz.question: You run an A/B test planned for 1000 samples. After 300 samples, the variant is winning with p=0.03. You stop the test early. This is:
options: A valid way to save time and money
Peeking — it inflates the false positive rate because the stopping rule wasn't pre-specified
quiz.explanation: This is peeking — checking results early and stopping when you see significance. The true Type I error rate becomes much higher than the nominal 5% because you gave yourself multiple chances to find 'significance.' Use fixed horizon or sequential testing with pre-specified stopping rules instead.

quiz.question: An experiment finds that a new trading signal improves returns by $8/trade (p=0.001). But the signal requires 500ms of compute per trade, a second data subscription ($50/mo), and only triggers 3 times per month. This is an example of:
options: A great trading signal — low p-value means it works
Statistical significance without practical significance — the costs outweigh the benefits
quiz.explanation: Statistical significance tells you the effect is real, not that it's useful. $8/trade × 3 trades/month = $24/month. Costs: $50 data subscription + computing resources. Net: -$26/month. Always ask: 'Is this effect large enough to matter in practice?' before deploying.

quiz.question: What's the key difference between Bonferroni correction and False Discovery Rate (FDR) control?
options: FDR controls the proportion of false discoveries while Bonferroni controls the family-wise error rate (probability of ANY false positive)
quiz.explanation: Bonferroni controls P(at least one false positive) — very strict, reduces power. FDR controls E(false discoveries / total discoveries) — less strict, more power to find real effects. FDR is preferred in modern research when you're exploring many hypotheses and want to discover real effects while limiting false ones.

nodes.name: Bayesian Statistics for Prediction & Trading
nodes.description: Bayes' theorem is the mathematical framework for updating beliefs with new evidence. Learn how to think like a Bayesian — the foundation of prediction markets, probabilistic forecasting, and rational decision-making under uncertainty.
keyConcepts: Bayes' theorem: P(H|E) = P(E|H) × P(H) / P(E)
Prior: your belief before seeing evidence
Likelihood: how probable the evidence is under each hypothesis
Posterior: your updated belief after seeing evidence
Bayesian vs frequentist: different interpretations of probability
Conjugate priors: when prior and posterior are the same distribution family
Bayesian A/B testing: probability that variant B beats variant A
Prediction markets as Bayesian aggregation engines
nodes.whyItMatters: Every trade, bet, and prediction you make is an exercise in Bayesian reasoning. Your prior is your current belief about ETH's price. The evidence is new information (Fed announcement, whale movement, on-chain data). Your posterior is your updated position. Prediction markets like Polymarket ARE Bayesian machines — prices represent the market's posterior probability after aggregating all available information. Without Bayesian thinking, you'll hold positions too long or exit too early. With it, you systematically update your beliefs in proportion to the strength of new evidence.
practiceProblems: You believe there's a 30% chance BTC will hit $100K in 2026. A credible analyst releases a report with strong evidence predicting $100K. The analyst is right 70% of the time and wrong 30% of the time. What's your updated probability?

A Polymarket contract shows 'Yes' at $0.65 (65% probability). You believe the true probability is 45%. How much conviction do you need in your own analysis to bet against the market? Model this as prior vs evidence.

You're A/B testing two trading signals. After 50 trades each: Signal A has 22 wins (44%), Signal B has 28 wins (56%). Model as Beta-Binomial conjugate. What's P(Signal B > Signal A)? How does this change with 200 trades each?

A rare disease affects 0.1% of the population. A test is 99% accurate. You test positive. Your doctor says '99% chance you have it.' Using Bayes, what's the actual probability? Why is the doctor wrong?

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Bayes' Theorem (Visual Guide)
resources.whyHelpful: 3Blue1Brown's signature visual explanation of Bayes' theorem with intuitive animations. The best introduction to Bayesian reasoning — makes the math click through geometric intuition.

resources.title: Bayesian A/B Testing (Evan Miller)
resources.whyHelpful: Evan Miller's practical guide to Bayesian A/B testing with the Beta-Binomial model. Includes a JavaScript calculator — plug in your data and see P(variant B beats A) instantly. The gold standard for comparing two strategies.

resources.title: Seeing Theory: Bayesian Inference (Interactive)
resources.whyHelpful: Brown University's interactive Bayesian visualizer — adjust the prior distribution, add evidence one observation at a time, and watch the posterior update in real time. Hands-on intuition building.

resources.title: StatLect: Bayesian Inference
resources.whyHelpful: StatLect's comprehensive guide to Bayesian inference — prior selection, posterior computation, and conjugate priors with full mathematical derivations. Good as a reference when you need the formulas.

workedExamples.problem: You trade ETH daily. Your prior belief is that ETH has a 60% chance of going up on any given day (based on long-term trend). You notice that on days when there's a positive Fed announcement, ETH goes up 80% of the time. Fed announcements happen on 10% of trading days. Today there's a positive Fed announcement. Using Bayes' theorem, what's your updated probability that ETH goes up today?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1: Define events. H = ETH goes up (the hypothesis). E = Positive Fed announcement (the evidence). Step 2: Prior P(H) = 0.60. Likelihood P(E|H) = P(Fed announcement on up days) = 0.10 (Fed announcements happen on 10% of all days, regardless — we need P(E|H)). Wait — the 80% refers to P(up | Fed announcement), which is P(H|E), not P(E|H). Let's re-read: 'On days with positive Fed announcement, ETH goes up 80% of the time' = P(H|E) = 0.80. But the 10% is P(E) = 0.10. And P(H) = 0.60. We need P(E|H) for Bayes. P(E|H) = P(H|E) × P(E) / P(H) = 0.80 × 0.10 / 0.60 = 0.1333. So Fed announcements happen on 13.33% of up days. Now apply Bayes: P(H|E) = P(E|H) × P(H) / P(E) = 0.1333 × 0.60 / 0.10 = 0.80 = 80%. Your posterior belief that ETH goes up today should be 80% — up from your prior of 60%. The evidence (Fed announcement) is strong enough to significantly shift your belief. This is why traders watch Fed announcements: they update probabilities by 20 percentage points.
workedExamples.explanation: Bayes' theorem quantifies exactly how much to update your beliefs. The key numbers: prior P(H)=60%, likelihood P(E|H)=13.33% (Fed announcements are more common on up days), evidence base rate P(E)=10%. The posterior P(H|E)=80% emerges naturally. If the evidence were weaker (e.g., P(E|H) close to P(E)), the posterior would barely move. This is why Bayesian thinking is so powerful: it tells you exactly how much weight to give new information.

workedExamples.problem: You're evaluating two trading strategies. Before seeing any data, you believe each strategy has a 50% chance of being profitable (your prior). You run Strategy A for 30 trades — it wins 18 times (60% win rate). Strategy B runs for 30 trades — wins 12 times (40% win rate). Model this with a Beta-Binomial conjugate prior. Use Beta(1,1) as your prior (uniform, reflecting complete uncertainty). What's the posterior distribution for each strategy's true win rate? What's the probability that Strategy A's true win rate exceeds 50%? What about Strategy B?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1: Beta-Binomial conjugacy. Prior: Beta(α=1, β=1). After observing s successes and f failures: Posterior: Beta(α+s, β+f). Step 2: Strategy A. s=18, f=12. Posterior: Beta(19, 13). Mean = 19/(19+13) = 19/32 = 0.594. P(true win rate > 50%): Using Beta CDF: P(X > 0.5) where X ~ Beta(19,13). From Beta(19,13) CDF tables or computation: P(X > 0.5) ≈ 0.853. An 85.3% probability that Strategy A's true win rate exceeds 50%. Step 3: Strategy B. s=12, f=18. Posterior: Beta(13, 19). Mean = 13/32 = 0.406. P(true win rate > 50%): P(X > 0.5) for Beta(13,19) ≈ 1 − 0.853 = 0.147. Only a 14.7% probability. Step 4: Probability that Strategy A > Strategy B. For two independent Beta variables: P(A > B) can be computed as: sum over k of P(A = exact value) × P(B < that value). For Beta(19,13) vs Beta(13,19): P(A > B) ≈ 0.91. There's a 91% probability that Strategy A has a higher true win rate than Strategy B. With only 30 trades each, we're not 100% sure, but the evidence is fairly strong. If you ran 200 trades each with the same proportions: A: Beta(121, 81), B: Beta(81, 121). P(A > B) ≈ 0.997 — nearly certain.
workedExamples.explanation: The Beta-Binomial conjugate prior is the most practical Bayesian tool for trading. It lets you update your belief about a probability (win rate, conversion rate, success rate) one trade at a time. Starting with Beta(1,1) (uniform prior) means you're maximally uncertain before seeing evidence. After each trade, you literally just add 1 to α (for a win) or β (for a loss). After 30 trades, you have a clear distribution. The key insight: with 30 trades each, there's 'only' 91% confidence that A is better than B. This is why experienced traders demand 100+ trades before committing to a strategy.

workedExamples.problem: A Polymarket contract is trading at $0.72 (72%) for 'BTC above $80K by December 2026.' Your own analysis (fundamentals, technicals, on-chain data) suggests a 55% probability. You have $500 to allocate. The market maker takes 2% on each trade (buy $0.72, sell $0.70, spread = $0.02). Should you bet against the market? Model this using Bayesian reasoning: treat the market price as a noisy signal. Assume the market's posterior incorporates information you might not have. How much weight should you give your own analysis vs the market price?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1: Bayesian model. Let T = 'BTC > $80K by Dec 2026' (the truth). Prior: P(T) = 0.55 (your analysis). Evidence: Market price = $0.72. We need P(T | market = $0.72). Step 2: Likelihood model. Assume the market is like an analyst: it has some accuracy. Historical data suggests prediction markets near expiration are ~90% accurate (prices within 5% of true outcome). Early markets (6+ months out) are ~65% accurate. Since this is 7 months out, let's say market accuracy around decision time ≈ 70%. Step 3: Apply Bayes. P(market at $0.72 | T is true). If BTC will be above $80K, what's the probability the market shows $0.72? Let's say: when the outcome is Yes, the market price is normally distributed around $0.75 with SD=$0.15. When No, price centered at $0.30 with SD=$0.15. P(market=$0.72 | T=True) ≈ density at 0.72 of N(0.75,0.15) ≈ 2.64. P(market=$0.72 | T=False) ≈ density at 0.72 of N(0.30,0.15) ≈ 0.01. Step 4: Posterior. P(T|market=$0.72) = (2.64 × 0.55) / (2.64 × 0.55 + 0.01 × 0.45) = 1.452 / 1.4565 ≈ 0.997. Your posterior is now 99.7%! But wait — this model gives the market too much weight because we assumed the market is perfectly rational. Real markets have noise, manipulation, and herding. Step 5: Add market noise. Instead of treating the price as precise evidence, add a noise factor. The true signal is: market_signal = true_probability + noise. If noise SD = 0.10, then: Likelihood ratio is much closer to 1. P(T|market=$0.72 with noise) ≈ 0.78. Your posterior is 78% — higher than your prior of 55%, but not 99.7%. Step 6: Decision. Expected value of buying Yes at $0.72: EV = 0.78 × ($1.00 - $0.72) × (1 - 0.02) + 0.22 × (-$0.72) × (1 - 0.02) = 0.78 × $0.2744 + 0.22 × (-$0.7056) = $0.214 - $0.155 = $0.059 per dollar bet. Positive EV (+5.9%). Buy. But with position sizing: Kelly criterion suggests betting ~6% of your bankroll = $30. Not your whole $500.
workedExamples.explanation: This is the most practical example — it directly models how a Bayesian trader uses prediction markets. The key insight: you should almost never bet against a prediction market price unless your analysis is VERY different from the market consensus AND you have high confidence in your model. The example shows that even with 55% vs 72% (a 17-point gap), after accounting for market noise, your edge might be only 5.9%. Bet small. Position size matters more than directional accuracy. Polymarket prices are remarkably efficient — most 'mispricings' your analysis finds are actually you being overconfident.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to update your prior when strong evidence arrives — a 20-point Fed announcement update (60% → 80%) is huge. If you don't adjust your positions accordingly, you're leaving edge on the table.

Being overconfident in your prior — if you start with 60% and dismiss all contradictory evidence, you're not doing Bayesian reasoning, you're cherry-picking. The whole point is to update in proportion to evidence strength.
Treating prediction market prices as 'ground truth' — they're noisy signals, not oracles. A $0.72 price is strong evidence but not proof. Always add noise to your likelihood model.

Confusing P(evidence | hypothesis) with P(hypothesis | evidence) — the prosecutor's fallacy. 'The test is 99% accurate' means P(positive | disease) = 0.99, NOT P(disease | positive) = 0.99. The actual P(disease | positive) can be much lower if the disease is rare.

Using a flat prior (Beta(1,1)) when you actually have strong prior beliefs — if you've been trading for 5 years and have 1000+ trades of data, your prior should reflect that experience, not uniform uncertainty.

quiz.question: You believe a coin is fair (50% heads). You flip it 10 times and get 8 heads. Using a Beta(1,1) prior, what's your posterior distribution for the coin's true probability of heads?
options: Beta(1, 10) — the coin is clearly biased
quiz.explanation: Beta-Binomial conjugacy: Prior Beta(α=1, β=1), after 8 successes and 2 failures: Posterior Beta(1+8, 1+2) = Beta(9, 3). Mean = 9/12 = 0.75 = 75%. Your belief shifted from 50% to 75% after seeing 8 heads in 10 flips.

quiz.question: A medical test for a rare disease is 99% accurate (99% true positive rate, 99% true negative rate). The disease affects 1 in 1000 people. You test positive. What's the probability you actually have the disease?
options: ~9% — most positives are false positives because the disease is rare
quiz.explanation: Apply Bayes: P(disease|positive) = (0.99 × 0.001) / (0.99 × 0.001 + 0.01 × 0.999) = 0.00099 / 0.01098 ≈ 0.090 = 9%. The disease is so rare that even a 99% accurate test produces mostly false positives. This is the classic 'base rate fallacy' that Bayes' theorem corrects.

quiz.question: A prediction market shows a contract at $0.65 (65% probability). Your prior is 50%. How should a Bayesian trader interpret this?
options: Ignore the market — your analysis is better
Update your posterior using Bayes: P(your analysis), P(market signal), and combine them
Bet everything against the market since there's a 15% gap
Assume the market is always right
quiz.explanation: A Bayesian trader treats the market price as evidence, not truth. Your prior (50%) and the market signal (65%) are both inputs to Bayes' theorem. The exact posterior depends on how much weight you give to each source of evidence. If the market has a strong track record (high likelihood), your posterior should move toward 65%. But it's a blend, not a jump. The gap (15%) is information, not a guaranteed arbitrage.

nodes.name: Bayes' Theorem for Trading Signals
nodes.description: Turn indicator alerts into updated probabilities. Use Bayes' theorem to ask whether a fired signal actually means a profitable trade — not just whether winners tend to trigger it.
keyConcepts: Bayes for signals: P(win | signal) = P(signal | win) × P(win) / P(signal)
Prior P(win): your base win rate before the alert
Likelihood P(signal | win) and P(signal | loss)
Posterior: updated probability after the signal fires
Prosecutor's fallacy: P(signal | win) is not P(win | signal)
False positives when base win rates are low
nodes.whyItMatters: Every RSI crossover, breakout alert, or whale-wallet ping is evidence — not a guarantee. Bayes tells you how much to trust a signal given your strategy's base win rate and how often the signal fires on winners vs losers. Traders who treat alerts as certainties overtrade; traders who update with Bayes size positions to the actual odds.
practiceProblems: Your strategy wins 45% of trades. A MACD crossover fires on 70% of winners but 40% of losers. MACD just crossed — what is P(win | signal)?
Same strategy, but the signal only fires 5% of the time on winners and 4% on losers. Does the signal change your belief much? Why or why not?
A backtest says "when RSI < 30, win rate is 62%." Your overall win rate is 48%. What prior should you use, and why is 62% alone misleading?
Explain in one sentence why a signal with 90% true-positive rate can still produce mostly losing trades after it fires.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Bayes' Theorem
resources.whyHelpful: Visual proof of Bayes' theorem — the same formula you'll plug trading numbers into. Watch this before working through signal examples.

resources.title: Conditional Probability Visualized
resources.whyHelpful: Builds intuition for P(A|B) vs P(B|A), the confusion that makes traders overtrust hot indicators.

resources.title: Bayesian Thinking for Traders
resources.whyHelpful: Short application of Bayesian updating to price action — connects the formula to live trading decisions.

quiz.question: Your strategy wins 40% of trades (prior). A breakout signal fires on 80% of winners and 50% of losers. The signal just fired. What is P(win | signal)?
quiz.explanation: P(signal) = 0.8×0.4 + 0.5×0.6 = 0.62. P(win|signal) = (0.8×0.4)/0.62 = 0.32/0.62 ≈ 51.6% ≈ 52%. The signal helps, but you are still wrong almost half the time.

quiz.question: A trader says: "This indicator is on 85% of my winning trades, so when it fires I have an 85% chance to win." What mistake are they making?
options: Confusing P(signal | win) with P(win | signal)
quiz.explanation: P(signal | win) = 85% means the indicator often appears on winners. P(win | signal) also depends on how often it fires on losers and your base win rate. These are not the same quantity.

quiz.question: Base win rate is 35%. A rare signal fires on 10% of winners and 8% of losers. After the signal fires, your posterior win probability is closest to:
quiz.explanation: P(signal) = 0.10×0.35 + 0.08×0.65 = 0.087. P(win|signal) = (0.10×0.35)/0.087 ≈ 0.402 ≈ 40%, closest to 38%. Weak, rare signals barely move a low prior.

quiz.question: In Bayes for trading signals, the prior P(win) should usually be:
options: Your strategy base win rate before the signal
The win rate only on days the signal fires
quiz.explanation: The prior is your belief about winning before seeing the signal — typically your historical base win rate. The signal updates that belief; it does not replace it.

workedExamples.problem: Your ETH swing strategy wins 45% of trades overall. A bullish RSI divergence signal appears on 60% of winners and 30% of losers. RSI divergence just triggered on a new setup. What is P(win | signal)? Should you take full size?
workedExamples.explanation: The signal is informative because it is much more common on winners (60%) than losers (30%). A 17-point jump is meaningful — but 62% still means roughly 2 in 5 trades lose. Size accordingly; do not go max leverage because "the signal fired."

workedExamples.problem: A Twitter alert claims "when funding goes negative, longs win 70% of the time." Your base long win rate is 50%. Historically, negative funding occurred on 55% of winning longs and 45% of losing longs. Negative funding just hit. What is the updated win probability?
workedExamples.explanation: The headline "70% win rate when funding negative" is a conditional slice, not your posterior from a 50% prior. With these likelihoods, funding only nudges you from 50% to 55%. Always rebuild with Bayes instead of trusting conditioned backtest stats as gospel.

workedExamples.problem: You test a new breakout scanner. Base win rate is 30% (hard market). The scanner fires on 90% of eventual winners but also on 85% of losers — it is noisy. Scanner alert on SOL. Compute P(win | alert). Is this scanner useful?
workedExamples.explanation: Despite catching 90% of winners, the scanner barely beats the 30% prior because it also fires on 85% of losers. High P(signal|win) alone is useless if P(signal|loss) is also high. Good signals separate winners from losers — wide likelihood gap, not just high sensitivity.

commonMistakes: Treating P(signal | win) as P(win | signal) — a '90% of winners had this pattern' stat is not a 90% chance to win.
Using the conditioned backtest win rate as your prior — the prior is the overall base rate; the signal updates it.
Ignoring how often the signal fires on losing trades — high false-positive rates destroy edge even with good sensitivity.
Abandoning a positive prior after one signal misfire — single outcomes are noise; Bayes updates incrementally.
